content: Concept Map Example We Want to Make Accessible

When we mean accessible, not just that screenreaders can read through all of the notes, but more that BVI are able to follow this level of clusters craeted by arrows, clusters etc. created by sighted users.
=> want to be able to translate the concept logics as less loss of information as possible to BVI + have them contribute as well
Applying a skill to a different, more complex, or real-world situation that is not obviously similar to the learning context.
Applying a skill to a situation that is very similar to the one in which it was learned
Learners can do with instruction and what they can do alone
Community Centered - Learning happens through interaction and collaboration.
Assesment Centered - Uses feedback and reflection to guide learning.
Knowledge Centered - Focuses on a strong understanding of content & concepts.
Learner Centered - Focuses on the learner’s needs, interests, and prior knowledge.

Characteristics of Experts - 

1. Deep, structured knowledge

2. Ability to recognize patterns quickly

3. Strong mental models
Experts - can reflexively retrieve information and build ideas from established concepts
Scratch - constructionist learning platform that enables students/learners to be hands-on, allowing for active learning and development.

Transfer - being able to transfer your learning from one context to another


(Far vs Near Transfer)
Learners. Who participates, and what particular traits do they have that are important for edtech design?]

Techniques:

modeling (instructor demonstrating task), hints and prompts, guided learning

equality vs equity
(policies such as no child left behind, etc. )
focus on surface features, rely on memorization
One Laptop per Child: initiative from MIT to inject laptops into low income countries, the thought being that giving access to computers will inherently improve education levels (technocentric) #%&$

Big B Behaviorism = all that matters is observable behavior
test final knowledge to gauge all learning done and determine any learning gaps (particularly with meeting benchmarks)
LOGO: early educational software designed to allow students to learn computing concepts
problematic because learners lack motiv when game element removed
Accessibility: some learners need different kinds of resources
Growth and improvement which are systems of reward
Without meaningful progression, no motivation for learning after removing game elements
Accommodate: Modifying existing schema for new information
formative assessments help reinforce concepts along the way as well as modify the student's understanding if their initial understanding is wrong
Assimilate: Fitting new knowledge into existing schema

Seymour Papert: father of constructionism, emphasized body knowledge, Objects to think with, and a rejection of traditional school. Believed computers were the ultimate OTTW. Criticized for being technocentric
operant conditioning: change behaviors via reward and punishment

Autonomy - sense of responsibility of ones progress
Relatability - sense of belonging in the learning environment
Mastery - Room for growth, challenge, and feedback
Learners who are motivated to gain new skills and experience
Engagement & Motivation. Who uses edtech, and why, and how?
Instructional design. What are some different ways of structuring lessons? Examples? Pros and cons?
Contexts [Where do we find and use learning technologies?]
Informal learning environments have different challenges compared to classroom design
Assessment, learner, knowledge, and community centered learning environments
Augmented and Virtual Reality -> Not fully proven and understood
Platforms. [What are the technologies that we use, and what are the affordances and drawbacks of each?]
